text.tspan: A:Let nums = [a1, a2, ... , an] be an array of
integers, let k be an integer representing the
target sum, and T be the total number of sub-
arrays of nums that sum to k.
Define the prefix sum sequence p0,p1,...,pn as
where pi represents the sum of the first i ele-
A subarray nums[l,...,r] where 1 ≤ l ≤ r ≤ n
This sum equals k if and only if
Then, we can compute pi for every item in nums
and store the number of times its value has been
"seen" using a hash map C, where C(pi) returns
the number of times the value pi has been seen.
If at any point we find a pi such that pi - k has
been seen (C(pi - k) > 0), it means that we have
found a pr and p(l-1) that satisfy
given by the fact that l ≤ r and C(pi - k) > 0.
In other words, we have found a subarray of nums
To compute T, the total number of subarrays that
sum to k, we make T = T + C(pi - k) everytime we
find a pi such that C(p_i - k) > 0. This accounts
for multiplicity as each occurrence of a particu-
lar prefix considers a valid subarray.
In Python this is implemented as:
A: Define a recursive function sumLeafNumbers that
recieves a Node object node and an integer c as
The function sumLeafNumbers should return 0 if
a it recieves a null node. Otherwise, it should
update the value of c as c = 10 * c + node.val
to help parse the number encoded between the root
of the tree and the current node.
Then, it should check if the current node is a
leaf, that means that its left and right pointers
are null. If this is the case, return c as this
is the number parsed from the root to the node.
If the node is not a leaf, recursively call
sumLeafNumbers on the left and right nodes from
the current node, passing the updated c as the
second argument. Add the results of both calls
This will add all the leaf numbers in the tree.
A Python implementation of sumLeafNumbers looks
if not node.left and not node.right:
This is a visualization of how
c, L, R, and L+R where updated
by the end of the process.
